danger_signals.visual_form: Monster and creature sprites derived from classic horror movie archetypes
danger_signals.meaning: Direct threat to player; enemies that must be engaged or avoided

danger_signals.visual_form: Detailed, cartoony grotesque environments across five thematic worlds
danger_signals.meaning: World state shifted toward supernatural danger; player location is hostile

danger_signals.visual_form: Detailed sprite work showing monster types from late-night horror movie aesthetics
danger_signals.meaning: Specific threat types communicated through creature silhouette and coloration

safety_signals.visual_form: Zeke and Julie sprites rendered with clear, recognizable character designs
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and continuity from Zombies Ate My Neighbors; familiar safe point-of-view

safety_signals.visual_form: Art direction balances threat with approachability
safety_signals.meaning: Game maintains comedic safety valve; horror is stylized, not realistic

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Protagonists represent human defenders against horror exhibit outbreak

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple monster types based on classic horror movie archetypes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Five thematic worlds suggest faction organization by horror subgenre (e.g., zombie, vampire, monster worlds implied)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Five distinct horror-themed worlds become playable environments; transition between worlds marks escalation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies spawn and patrol according to world theme; creature behavior varies by horror type

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cartoony detailed graphics depicting grotesque horror scenarios across multiple themed zones
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must traverse worlds sequentially to save town; increasing danger implied through world progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environment transitions mark world changes; detailed sprite work shows thematic shifts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: 360-degree shoot-em-up action required; player must engage threats in all directions

environmental_storytelling.example: Five distinct horror-themed worlds communicate that horror exhibit is organized, multi-faceted threat; each world visual identity reinforces specific horror subgenre

Detailed sprites and backgrounds depict horror concepts (monsters, dark environments) in stylized rather than realistic manner; visual language communicates 'late-night horror movie' tone rather than survival horror
Zeke and Julie returning as protagonists visually connects to Zombies Ate My Neighbors and implies ongoing threat to their town across multiple incidents

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Enemies appear across all five worlds with behavior patterns tied to horror archetype; 360-degree threat model suggests creatures attack from any angle
Protagonists engage in run-and-gun action; capable of shooting in multiple directions to address 360-degree enemy threat
Platforms and terrain present additional danger beyond enemies; detailed backgrounds suggest complex level design with multiple threat vectors

notes: Ghoul Patrol is a licensed sequel to Zombies Ate My Neighbors, originally developed as an unrelated game. The visual communication strategy relies heavily on cartoony grotesque aesthetics to maintain comedic tone while presenting genuine horror threats. The game's manifestation layer communicates world-state primarily through thematic environmental design (five horror worlds) and creature archetypes rather than through explicit UI. Reviewed positively for '360-degree shoot-em-up action' and 'detailed, cartoony graphics,' indicating visual clarity is key to communication. Sources provide minimal detail on specific visual signals; T3 tier designation and available sources limit precision on faction identity systems and world-state visual indicators.
